task: I’ve got a routine procedure coming up, and I want to be sure I’m not forgetting anything basic before and after. Could you help me make a simple checklist of what to ask, what to have ready at home, and what signs would mean I should call the doctor rather than just wait it out?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a plain checklist you can use.

## Before the procedure: what to ask
- What should I stop taking, and when?
- Can I eat or drink beforehand?
- Will I need a ride home?
- What pain, swelling, bleeding, or side effects are normal?
- What should I do at home afterward?
- What would mean I should call right away?
- When should I follow up?

## What to have ready at home
- Prescribed medicines filled
- Usual medicines checked with instructions
- Ice pack or heating pad, if recommended
- Clean gauze or bandages, if needed
- Water and easy food
- Phone charger
- Someone’s phone number in case you need help
- A quiet place to rest
- Any instructions from the doctor in one place

## What to keep nearby
- Discharge instructions
- Prescription list
- Doctor’s phone number
- Pharmacy number
- Emergency contact
- Insurance card, if needed for follow-up

## Call the doctor if you have
- Bleeding that won’t stop
- Fever
- Worsening pain instead of improving
- Swelling that gets worse
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- Dizziness or fainting
- Redness, pus, or bad smell at the site
- Trouble eating, drinking, urinating, or moving as expected
- Anything the instructions say is not normal

## Simple rule
If it’s getting worse instead of better, or it feels different from what they told you to expect, call.

If you want, I can turn this into a short one-page checklist you can print or copy by hand.

user_simulator.text: That’s useful, but I’d want it a little tighter and more practical. For example, a section for the night before, the morning of, and the first 24 hours after would be easier to follow. And I’d like the call-the-doctor part to be a bit more specific about what’s urgent versus what can wait until office hours. I’ve learned the hard way that “just watch it” can turn into a long night.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version, broken into **night before**, **morning of**, **first 24 hours**, and **when to call**.

## Night before
- Read the instructions once more
- Stop any medicines only if they told you to
- Don’t eat or drink if you were told not to
- Set out clothes, ID, insurance card, and paperwork
- Charge your phone
- Arrange a ride if needed
- Put water, easy food, and prescriptions where you can reach them
- Clear a place to rest when you get home

## Morning of
- Follow the fasting or medicine instructions exactly
- Take only the medicines they said were okay
- Don’t wear jewelry if they told you not to
- Bring:
  - ID
  - insurance card
  - medicine list
  - allergies
  - paperwork and instructions
  - phone and charger
- Leave early enough not to rush
- Ask again about:
  - what is normal afterward
  - what is not normal
  - who to call after hours

## First 24 hours after
- Rest
- Take medicines as directed
- Eat and drink as allowed
- Keep the site clean and dry if that applies
- Use ice, heat, or bandages only as instructed
- Don’t drive, lift, or do too much until you know it’s okay
- Keep the discharge paper handy
- Check the area or symptoms as often as they told you to

## Call the doctor during office hours if
- Pain is not improving, but it is not severe
- Mild swelling or redness is lasting longer than expected
- You have questions about medicines or care
- You are unsure whether a symptom is normal
- You missed a dose or were unsure what to take

## Call right away / urgent if
- Bleeding that won’t stop
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- Fainting or near-fainting
- Severe pain
- Rapidly increasing swelling
- Fever with worsening symptoms
- Pus, bad smell, or spreading redness
- Repeated vomiting or can’t keep fluids down
- New confusion or severe weakness
- Anything the instructions say is an emergency

## Simple rule
- **Office hours:** not getting better, but stable
- **Urgent:** worse fast, severe, or hard to breathe / think / stay awake

If you want, I can make this into a very short checkbox version you can print and keep with the discharge papers.

user_simulator.text: That’s closer. Still a little too broad in the urgent part — I’d want a clearer split between what means call the surgeon or office the same day, and what means ER / 911. And for the first 24 hours, I’d like a couple of plain reminders about meals, pain medicine, and whether it’s okay to be alone. After a procedure, that’s the part people gloss over, and it matters.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a clearer split.

## Night before
- Read the instructions all the way through
- Stop medicines only if they told you to
- Don’t eat or drink if you were told not to
- Set out:
  - ID
  - insurance card
  - medicine list
  - allergies
  - paperwork
  - phone and charger
- Arrange a ride if needed
- Put water, easy food, and prescriptions where you can reach them
- Make a place to rest when you get home

## Morning of
- Follow fasting and medicine instructions exactly
- Take only the medicines they said were okay
- Bring your paperwork and a written question list
- Leave early enough not to rush
- Ask:
  - what is normal today
  - what is not normal
  - who to call after hours
  - whether you should be alone or have someone stay with you

## First 24 hours
- Rest
- Eat and drink as allowed
- Take pain medicine exactly as directed
- Don’t mix medicines unless they said it was okay
- Don’t drive, drink alcohol, lift, or do chores until cleared
- Keep the area clean and dry if that applies
- Keep the discharge instructions nearby
- If they said you should not be alone, have someone stay with you

## Call the office or surgeon the same day if
- Pain is not severe, but it is not improving
- Mild swelling, redness, or bruising is lasting longer than expected
- You have questions about medicine doses or timing
- You missed a dose or are unsure what to take
- You have a low fever, but otherwise feel okay
- You are not sure whether a symptom is normal

## Call right away / go to ER or 911 if
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- Fainting or cannot stay awake
- Heavy bleeding that won’t stop
- Sudden severe pain
- Rapid swelling
- Blue lips, severe weakness, or confusion
- Repeated vomiting and can’t keep fluids down
- Signs of a bad allergic reaction:
  - swelling of face, lips, or tongue
  - hives
  - wheezing
- Any symptom the discharge sheet calls an emergency

## Simple rule
- **Same day office call:** concerning, but stable
- **ER / 911:** severe, sudden, breathing-related, chest-related, or not staying awake

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page checkbox list with just the essentials.

user_simulator.text:
That’s better, yes. Still want it a hair more usable — the kind of thing I can glance at without hunting around.

Could you make the same-day vs ER part a little more concrete with a few examples? Like:
- same day: fever under a certain point, redness that’s not spreading, pain meds not lasting long enough
- ER/911: bleeding through bandages, one-sided weakness, trouble breathing, chest pain, can’t wake up, that sort of thing

And on the first 24 hours, I’d like one plain line on meals — light food if tolerated, don’t force it, that kind of thing — and one on being alone. Some procedures, fine to be by yourself; some, not so much. The discharge papers don’t always spell that out clearly enough.

I’m after something I could keep on the counter and not have to interpret when I’m tired.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more glanceable version.

## Night before
- Read the instructions once through
- Stop medicines only if they told you to
- Don’t eat or drink if you were told not to
- Set out:
  - ID
  - insurance card
  - medicine list
  - allergies
  - paperwork
  - phone and charger
- Arrange a ride if needed
- Put water, easy food, and prescriptions within reach
- Make a place to rest when you get home

## Morning of
- Follow fasting and medicine instructions exactly
- Take only the medicines they said were okay
- Bring your paperwork and a written question list
- Leave early enough not to rush
- Ask:
  - what is normal today
  - what is not normal
  - who to call after hours
  - whether you should be alone afterward or have someone stay with you

## First 24 hours
- Rest
- Eat light food if you feel like it; don’t force meals
- Drink as allowed
- Take pain medicine exactly as directed
- Don’t mix medicines unless they said it was okay
- Don’t drive, drink alcohol, lift, or do chores until cleared
- Keep the area clean and dry if that applies
- Keep the discharge instructions nearby
- If they said you should not be alone, have someone stay with you

## Call the office or surgeon the same day if
- Fever is **under 100.4°F** but you otherwise feel okay
- Redness is mild and **not spreading**
- Swelling or bruising is mild but not improving
- Pain medicine wears off too soon, but pain is still manageable
- You missed a dose or are unsure what to take next
- You have questions about wound care, bathing, or activity
- You’re not sure whether a symptom is normal

## Call right away / go to ER or 911 if
- Bleeding soaks through bandages or won’t stop
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- Fainting or can’t stay awake
- One-sided weakness, facial droop, or trouble speaking
- Sudden severe pain
- Rapidly increasing swelling
- Blue lips, severe weakness, or confusion
- Repeated vomiting and can’t keep fluids down
- Signs of a bad allergic reaction:
  - swelling of face, lips, or tongue
  - hives
  - wheezing
- Any symptom the discharge sheet calls an emergency

## Simple rule
- **Same day office call:** stable, but not quite right
- **ER / 911:** severe, sudden, breathing-related, chest-related, bleeding heavily, or neurologic changes

If you want, I can trim this into a one-page checkbox sheet with just the essentials.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the shape of it. The fever line helps, and the same-day vs ER split is clearer now. I’d still want it a little more plainspoken and less medical-sounding if you can — something I could glance at and not have to think twice about.

A couple things I’d still tighten:
- For the same-day section, maybe say “temperature under 100.4 if that’s the number they gave you” instead of making it sound fixed for every procedure.
- For ER/911, I’d like “bleeding through the bandage” and “can’t wake up” to stand out even more. Those are the ones people second-guess, and they shouldn’t.
- On being alone, maybe one short line that says: “If they told you not to be alone, don’t plan to tough it out.” Plain enough.

If you want to make it truly counter-top simple, I’d take one more pass with fewer words and a little more white space.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the simpler, more plainspoken version.

## Night before
- Read the instructions once
- Stop medicines only if they told you to
- Don’t eat or drink if they said not to
- Set out:
  - ID
  - insurance card
  - medicine list
  - allergies
  - paperwork
  - phone and charger
- Arrange a ride if needed
- Put water, easy food, and prescriptions within reach
- Make a place to rest when you get home

## Morning of
- Follow the instructions exactly
- Take only the medicines they said were okay
- Bring your papers and a list of questions
- Leave early enough not to rush
- Ask:
  - what is normal today
  - what is not normal
  - who to call after hours
  - whether you should be alone afterward

## First 24 hours
- Rest
- Eat light food if you feel like it; don’t force it
- Drink as allowed
- Take pain medicine exactly as directed
- Don’t mix medicines unless they said it was okay
- Don’t drive, drink alcohol, lift, or do chores until cleared
- Keep the area clean and dry if that applies
- Keep the discharge instructions nearby
- If they told you not to be alone, don’t plan to tough it out

## Call the office or surgeon the same day if
- Temperature is **under 100.4°F, if that’s the number they gave you**
- Redness is mild and not spreading
- Swelling or bruising is mild but not improving
- Pain medicine wears off too soon, but the pain is still manageable
- You missed a dose or aren’t sure what to take next
- You have questions about wound care, bathing, or activity
- You’re not sure whether a symptom is normal

## Call right away / go to ER or 911 if
- **Bleeding soaks through the bandage**
- **You can’t wake up or stay awake**
- Trouble breathing
- Chest pain
- One-sided weakness, facial droop, or trouble speaking
- Sudden severe pain
- Rapidly increasing swelling
- Blue lips, severe weakness, or confusion
- Repeated vomiting and can’t keep fluids down
- Signs of a bad allergic reaction:
  - swelling of face, lips, or tongue
  - hives
  - wheezing
- Any symptom the discharge sheet calls an emergency

## Simple rule
- **Same day:** concerning, but steady
- **ER / 911:** heavy bleeding, breathing trouble, chest pain, can’t wake up, or sudden neurologic changes

If you want, I can turn this into a very short checkbox version with even more white space.

user_simulator.text:
That does it. Plain enough, and the two things I wanted to stand out do stand out.

I can use this.
